coredata.dc:title: Efficacy of a novel graphene quantum dots nanofluid in a microchannel heat exchanger
coredata.dc:creator: Jamshidmofid, Mohammad
Abbassi, Abbas
Bahiraei, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: Utilizing nanofluids is a suitable method to improve the performance of microchannel heat exchangers. Moreover, graphene quantum dots nanofluid, as a novel nanofluid with its unique features, can be a proper choice for employment in heat exchangers. In this article, for the first time, a graphene quantum dots nanofluid is employed as the working fluid of a microchannel heat exchanger. For this purpose, the graphene quantum dots nanofluid at concentrations of 0–0.5% and Reynolds numbers ranging from 50 to 200 is utilized as the hot fluid, while pure water with a Reynolds number of 100 is considered as the cold fluid. The problem-solving approach is the 3D numerical method based on the finite-volume technique. Moreover, temperature-dependent thermophysical properties are assumed for the hot and cold fluids. The results reveal that at Reynolds numbers of 50 and 100, utilizing the nanofluid greatly improves the heat transfer rate for a slight increase in the pressure drop, so that in the case of using the 0.5% nanofluid at Reynolds number of 50, the heat transfer rate improves 14% for only an 8% increase in the pressure drop. However, at Reynolds numbers of 150 and 200, the use of the nanofluid imposes a large pressure drop on the system. Based on the obtained results, utilizing the graphene quantum dots nanofluid at Reynolds numbers of 50 and 100 is suggested since the heat exchanger performance enhances, while at Reynolds numbers of 150 and 200, using the nanofluid is not recommended due to the high pressure drop penalty.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heat exchanger
Microchannel
Nanofluid
Graphene quantum dots
Effectiveness

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Definition of the geometry and working fluid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the microchannel heat exchanger and its geometrical parameters.
The mesh pattern for the microchannel heat exchanger.
Comparing the results of the current simulation with those reported by Zhou et al. [7] for (a) pressure drop and (b) temperature profile.
(a) Streamlines and velocity vectors and (b) velocity contour at Re = 200 and φ = 0.5%.
Contours of the (a) temperature, (b) thermal conductivity, and (c) viscosity of the nanofluid at Re = 200 and concentration of 0.5%.
The heat transfer rate in the heat exchanger at various Re values and concentrations.
Velocity profile for the warm and cool liquids at the streamwise direction for different concentrations at Re = 200.
Flow velocity in the microchannels at Re = 200 and φ = 0.5% for (a) hot nanofluid, (b) cold water.
Streamlines of the hot nanofluid at φ = 0.5% for (a) Re = 50, (b) Re = 100, (c) Re = 150, and (d) Re = 200.
Temperature profile for the warm and cold liquids at the streamwise direction for different concentrations at Re = 200.
Contour of temperature for the hot fluid at Re = 200 and concentrations of (a) 0, (b) 0.1%, (c) 0.3%, and (d) 0.5%.

Iso-temperature surfaces of the hot and cold fluids for φ = 0.5% at (a) Re = 50, hot-fluid (b) Re = 100, hot-fluid, (c) Re = 200, hot-fluid, (d) Re = 50, cold-fluid, (e) Re = 100, cold-fluid and (f) Re = 200, cold-fluid.
Iso-temperature surfaces of the warm and cold fluids for Re = 200 at (a) φ = 0, hot-fluid (b) φ = 0.5%, hot-fluid, (c) φ = 0, cold-fluid, (d) φ = 0.5%, cold-fluid.
Temperature contours in the cupric solid parts of the heat exchanger at (a) Re = 50 and φ = 0, (b) Re = 50 and φ = 0.5%, (c) Re = 200 and φ = 0 and (d) Re = 200 and φ = 0.5%.
Overall heat transfer coefficient against the Re at various concentrations.
Effectiveness of the heat exchanger vs the Re at varied concentrations.
Pressure contours of the nanofluid flow at (a) Re = 50 and φ = 0, (b) Re = 200 and φ = 0, (c) Re = 50 and φ = 0.5% and (d) Re = 200 and φ = 0.5%.
The pressure drop of the cold fluid at various Re values and concentrations.
Performance index at various Re values and concentrations.
The amounts of the geometrical quantities.
Models of thermal conductivity and viscosity achieved from the experimental data [29].
The constant amounts of thermophysical properties for the water, nanoparticles, and solid cupric wall [30].
Results of the mesh independency.
Temperature change [K] in the hot and cold fluids at various Re and concentrations.
The values of the NTU at various Re values and concentrations.
Values of the pressure loss [Pa] of the whole heat exchanger at different Re values and concentrations.

head.ce:title: Efficacy of a novel graphene quantum dots nanofluid in a microchannel heat exchanger

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Amirkabir University of Technology, Hafez Ave, P.O. Box 15916-34311, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Amirkabir University of Technology, Hafez Ave, P.O. Box 15916-34311, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Engineering, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilizing nanofluids is a suitable method to improve the performance of microchannel heat exchangers. Moreover, graphene quantum dots nanofluid, as a novel nanofluid with its unique features, can be a proper choice for employment in heat exchangers. In this article, for the first time, a graphene quantum dots nanofluid is employed as the working fluid of a microchannel heat exchanger. For this purpose, the graphene quantum dots nanofluid at concentrations of 0–0.5% and Reynolds numbers ranging from 50 to 200 is utilized as the hot fluid, while pure water with a Reynolds number of 100 is considered as the cold fluid. The problem-solving approach is the 3D numerical method based on the finite-volume technique. Moreover, temperature-dependent thermophysical properties are assumed for the hot and cold fluids. The results reveal that at Reynolds numbers of 50 and 100, utilizing the nanofluid greatly improves the heat transfer rate for a slight increase in the pressure drop, so that in the case of using the 0.5% nanofluid at Reynolds number of 50, the heat transfer rate improves 14% for only an 8% increase in the pressure drop. However, at Reynolds numbers of 150 and 200, the use of the nanofluid imposes a large pressure drop on the system. Based on the obtained results, utilizing the graphene quantum dots nanofluid at Reynolds numbers of 50 and 100 is suggested since the heat exchanger performance enhances, while at Reynolds numbers of 150 and 200, using the nanofluid is not recommended due to the high pressure drop penalty.

ce:section.ce:para: As devices for exchanging energy between two or more fluids, heat exchangers are an inherent part of various industrial units such as power plants, electronic systems, food and drug industries, and many other sectors that deal with the heat transfer process. Due to the extensive scope of work and the critical role of heat exchangers, research on these devices has always been critical for scholars. Recently, with the expansion of modern science and technology and the tendency toward the miniaturization of equipment, the study of miniature heat exchangers is one of the essential topics [1]. In this regard, several researchers have focused on evaluating the miniature heat exchangers and have proposed various solutions to promote the performance of this equipment. In fact, since reducing the size of heat exchangers reduces its heat capacity, scholars suggested two strategies to overcome this problem: geometrical modification and working fluid improvement.

So far, various modification techniques have been suggested to upgrade the geometrical structure of miniature heat exchangers. Some of these methods include employing geometrical obstacles such as twisted tapes, baffles, and winglets or creating rotation by curving the flow bed [2–5]. Through this method, the flow mixing is augmented by forming local vortices, leading to heat transfer enhancement. However, the major problem of this method is that besides the severe increase in the required pumping power and the weight of the heat exchanger, the formation of local vortices applies extra fluctuations to heat exchangers, which can cause damages in the long run and reduce the lifetime [2]. In addition to the obstacles, utilizing microchannel structures for the flow passage is another geometrical modification for improving the heat transfer rate. The main idea of this method is related to a paper published by Tuckerman and Pease [6] in 1981. In this paper, the authors concluded that as the hydraulic diameter of a duct decreases, the convective heat transfer coefficient enhances, leading to reduced thermal resistance. Therefore, in a heat exchanger, utilizing microchannel structures for the flow passage can drastically improve thermal performance. So far, numerous scholars have utilized microchannel structures in their studies. Zhou et al. [7] conducted a geometrical study of a microchannel heat exchanger and presented the optimal values of different geometrical parameters for the better performance of the heat exchanger. Zuo et al. [8], by investigating a three-layer microchannel heat sink at different flow configurations and different tapered geometries, introduced the cases with the lowest thermal resistance. Ling et al. [9] designed a new interdict microchannel heat exchanger and optimized geometrical parameters of the microchannel, including the width, depth, and spacing, to achieve better thermal performance. They reported that the interdict structure enhances the Nusselt number up to 64% compared to the parallel structure. In an experimental research, Sun et al. [10] analyzed and compared the heat transfer, mass transfer, and pressure drop of a desiccant coated microchannel heat exchanger (DMHE) and a desiccant coated fin-and-tube heat exchanger (DFHE). They concluded that the heat transfer, mass transfer, and pressure drop of the DMHE respectively were 50%, 15%, and 125% higher than those of the DFHE.

Another abovementioned method for improving the thermal features of miniature heat exchangers is to employ better coolants. Indeed, conventional heat transfer fluids such as water and various oils do not possess favorable thermal conductivity, and this can be a significant limitation for further improving the performance of heat exchangers. One feasible way to enhance the thermal conductivity of working fluid is to suspend high-thermal conductivity solid nanoparticles to the base fluid. This method was the idea of Choi and Eastman [11], who first used the word “nanofluid” for describing liquid-solid suspension, introducing a new generation of heat transfer fluid. Since the advent of nanofluids, lots of researchers have concentrated on these novel heat transfer fluids. The research studies in this field include utilizing nanofluids in heat exchangers [12], heat sinks [13–15], photovoltaic thermal collectors [16–20], and many other cases [21–23]. A common outstanding result in most of these studies is the improvement of thermal systems in the case of employing nanofluids. For instance, Bahiraei et al. [15] reported a 17% enhancement in the convective heat transfer coefficient using a 0.1% hybrid silver–graphene nanofluid in a microchannel heat sink embedding with ribs and secondary channels. Also, Sarlak et al. [14] declared a 14% improvement in the thermal performance of a microchannel heat sink in the case of utilizing a non-Newtonian multi-wall carbon nanotube. These results indicate the success of nanofluids in improving thermal performance. It should be noted that although the use of nanofluids enhances the thermal features, it also increases the amount of pressure drop. Therefore, judging the suitability of using nanofluids requires considering hydrodynamic parameters along with thermal parameters.

To date, various kinds of nanoparticles such as metal, metal oxides, and carbon-based structures have been utilized as the secondary phase of nanofluids [24,25]. Graphene, as a single-atom-thick-sheet of hexagonally arranged carbon atoms with a macroscale transverse size, is one of the carbon-based structures that has a thermal conductivity of about 5000 times higher than that of water [26]. Although the high thermal conductivity of graphene makes it a unique material for employing in nanofluids, the existence of large transverse dimensions reduces the Brownian motion and the stability of graphene nanosheets in the base fluid. Utilizing surfactants in the preparation of graphene nanofluids is one of the methods proposed by researchers to overcome this problem. Although surfactants can improve the stability of additive phases, they affect the thermal features of nanofluids and decrease their thermal conductivity [26]. Another method is to reduce the transverse dimensions of graphene to nanoscale. By implementing this strategy, the Brownian motion and the stability of graphene in the base fluid enhance without the need for surfactants. A new structure obtained by reducing the transverse dimensions of graphene to the nanoscale is called Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs). Up to now, there are few research studies dealing with the preparation of GQDs nanofluids. Ettefaghi et al. [27] produced a radiator coolant-based GQDs nanofluid using citric acid and urea and declared 5.2% and 17% improvements in the thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer coefficient for a 100 ppm concentration. Amiri et al. [28], by preparing and evaluating the stability of an amine-treated aqueous GQDs nanofluid, claimed that there is no sedimentation after 30 days. Sedaghat and Yousefi [29] produced a GQDs nanofluid with diverse base fluids and reported 53%, 21%, and 18% improvements in the thermal conductivity in the case of adding 0.5% GQDs to water, ethylene glycol, and water–ethylene glycol mixture, respectively.

Despite extensive research studies on microchannel heat exchangers, few scholars have utilized nanofluids as the working fluid in this equipment. The result of the authors' inquiry from the Scopus database demonstrates that up to 10 December 2020, there are more than 650 research items in the field of the microchannel heat exchanger while only less than 25 of them (about four percent) are related to utilizing nanofluids in microchannel heat exchangers. Moreover, there is no published document in the field of employing GQDs as the working fluid of heat exchangers. This is while the use of nanofluids in microchannel heat exchangers for increasing the heat transfer rate is even more important than other types of heat exchangers since owing to the presence of very small hydraulic diameters in these heat exchangers, other methods such as increasing the Reynolds number can lead to major problems like severe pressure drop. Also, due to the unique characteristics of GQDs, using this type of nanofluid can be a proper choice to achieve better thermal features of the working fluid. Considering the lack of sufficient studies, in this paper, the fluid flow and heat transfer characteristics of a microchannel heat exchanger working with newly developed aqueous GQDs nanofluid are addressed to get a clear understanding of the suitability of using this novel nanofluid in the microchannel heat exchanger.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Definition of the geometry and working fluid
ce:section.ce:para: The present geometry is a cupric counterflow microchannel heat exchanger that is depicted in Fig. 1 with its relevant geometrical parameters. In the revealed hot fluid side, water or nanofluid with a specific volume concentration flows, while the cold fluid side relates to water. Each fluid enters the configuration from the illustrated regions, passes through the microchannels, and finally leaves the heat exchanger from the two output areas embedded at the end of each geometry. There are 29 microchannels in the middle part of each configuration in which the main flow is divided between them, and the heat transfer process occurs through the cupric solid parts from the warm fluid to the cool one. The cross-sectional shape of these microchannels is rectangular with the dimensions of 1.25 × 0.5 mm. The magnitudes of all the geometrical parameters, which have been displayed in Fig. 1, are listed in Table 1 . It should be noted that these dimensions have been chosen based on Ref. [7] in a way that for the highest heat transfer, the lowest pressure drop is achieved.

Two different fluid types are considered as the working fluid of the heat exchanger. For the hot side of the heat exchanger, water or aqueous GQDs nanofluid with the concentrations of 0.1%, 0.3%, and 0.5% flows, while the cold side relates to the water. GQDs are a kind of 0-dimension carbon-based structures that have a high potential for utilization in nanofluids due to their unique features such as high thermal conductivity, large surface area, high stability, simple preparation method, and low cost. The method of preparing GQDs dots nanofluid is presented in detail in an article published by Sedaghat and Yousefi [29]. In this method, the GQDs are synthesized by the chemical vapor deposition method using nitric acid and active carbon. After synthesizing GQDs, the nanofluid containing these particles is produced at different volume concentrations.

For assessing the thermohydraulic attributes of the counterflow plate microchannel heat exchanger, the following assumptions have been adopted:
•
Incompressible water and nanofluid flows
•
Laminar water and nanofluid flows (because the Re is low enough)
•
Steady-state heat transfer and fluid flow (because there is no destabilizing factor)
•
Single-phase and homogenous water and nanofluid flows
•
Neglecting radiation heat transfer
•
Neglecting viscous heating
•
Neglecting body forces
•
Constant solid properties
The governing equations are as follows:

Continuity:
(1)
∇ · ( ρ
V
) = 0

Momentum:
(2)
∇ · ( ρ
VV
) = - ∇ P + ∇ · ( μ ∇
V
)

Energy:
(3)
∇ ·
( ρ
V
c p
T )
= ∇ ·
( k ∇ T )

The symbols ρ, cp , k, and μ refer to the density, specific heat, thermal conductivity, and dynamic viscosity, respectively. Also, P indicates the pressure, V represents the velocity, and T refers to the temperature.

Besides the governing equations for the fluid part, the equation describing the conduction heat transfer in the solid part of the heat exchanger is described as follows:
(4)
∇ 2
T = 0
Since the mean free path of fluid is much smaller than the microchannel width, the Knudsen number is far less than unity, ensuring the continuum assumption for the fluid flow.

The thermal conductivity and viscosity for the warm fluid (nanofluid) and cool fluid (water) are assumed as temperature-dependent. For this purpose, the experimental results of the water–GQDs nanofluid at different concentrations reported by Sedaghat and Yousefi [29] have been utilized, and proper mathematical functions have been fitted to them. The outcomes of this process are depicted in Table 2 . It is noteworthy that all units of quantities in this table are based on the SI system.

Because the density and specific heat do not change a lot with temperature, these properties are assumed constant. For the nanofluid, the values of density and specific heat, at a particular concentration, are achieved by Eqs. (5) and (6), respectively:
(5)
ρ
nf
= φ
ρ p
+
( 1 - φ )
ρ f
(6)
( ρ
c p
)
nf
= φ
( ρ
c p
)
p
+
( 1 - φ )
( ρ
c p
)
f

In these equations, φ refers to volume concentration, and the subscripts f and p relate to the base fluid (water) and nanoparticles, respectively. The constant amounts of thermophysical properties for water, nanoparticles, and solid cupric wall have been listed in Table 3 .

The heat transfer rates for the warm and cool fluids, obtained from the first law of thermodynamics, are as follows:
(7)
q h
=
m ̇
h
c
p , n f
(
T
h , i n

h , o u t
)
(8)
q c
=

c

p , w

c , i n
)

In the above equations, Th,in and Th,out denote the inlet and outlet temperatures of the warm fluid, respectively, while Tc,in and Tc,out are the inlet and outlet temperatures of the cold fluid. Also,
m ̇
h
and
m ̇
c
represent the mass flow rates of the warm and cold fluids, respectively. Additionally, the subscript w in Eq. (8) refers to the water. It should be noted that since there is no energy loss, the amounts of heat transfer rates for the hot and cold fluids are equivalent.

The overall heat transfer coefficient is determined via Eq. (9):
(9)
U =
q
A Δ
T
LMTD
whereas the symbol A indicates the heat transfer area, and ΔTLMTD stands for the logarithmic mean temperature difference, which is given as follows:
(10)
Δ
T
LMTD
=
Δ
T 2
- Δ
T 1
ln
Δ

Δ
T 1
(11)
Δ
T 1
=

h , i n

T
c , o u t
(12)
Δ
T 2
=
T
h , o u t
-
T
c , i n

To assess the effectiveness of the heat exchanger, the following equation is adopted:
(13)
ε =
q
q max

in which
q max
is the maximum possible heat transfer rate that is described through the following equation:
(14)
q max
=
C min
(
T
h , i n
-
T
c , i n
)

In Eq. (14),
C min
refers to the minimum heat capacity rate, which is specified as follows:
(15)
C min
= min
[
C h
,
C c
]

Ch and Cc indicate the heat capacity rates for the warm and cool fluids, respectively, which are defined through the following equations:
(16)
C h
=
m ̇
h
c
p , n f
(17)
C c
=
m ̇
c
c
p , w
Since Cmin can relate to the hot or cold fluid, the effectiveness has one of the two following relations:

If Cmin = Ch
(18)
ε =
T
h , i n
-
T
h , o u t
T
h , i n
-
T
c , i n

Otherwise, if Cmin = Cc
(19)
ε =
T
c , o u t
-
T
c , i n
T
h , i n
-
T
c , i n

Another critical parameter is the Number of Transfer Units (NTU), which has the following formulation:
(20)
NTU =
UA
C min

Finally, for evaluating the overall performance of the heat exchanger, the parameter named “performance index” is utilized:
(21)
η =
q /
q 0
Δ P
/
Δ
P 0
in which subscript 0 relates to the base fluid.

The uniform temperature and velocity profiles have been assumed at the entrances of both the warm and cool liquids. The hot fluid enters the configuration at the temperature of 313 K, while the cold fluid’s inlet temperature is 30 K less than the hot fluid’s one with the amount of 283 K. Since this simulation is carried out at constant Reynolds numbers (i.e., Re = 100 for the cold fluid and Re = 50, 100, 150, and 200 for the hot fluid), the inlet velocities of the warm and cold fluids are evaluated via the definition of Reynolds number (Re) as follows:
(22)
Re =
ρ
V
D h
μ

In Eq. (22), Dh has the below definition:
(23)
D h
=
4 A
P
where A and P respectively denote cross-sectional area and wetted perimeter of the cross-section.

It should be noted that the viscosity in Eq. (22) is considered as the average viscosity along the middle channel. At the outlets, both the warm and cool liquids exit with the atmosphere pressure (i.e., Pgauge = 0). Also, the adiabatic condition is assumed at the external walls of the heat exchanger, while for the internal walls that are exposed to the flow, the no-slip boundary condition is adopted.

In this 3D numerical evaluation, the governing equations are discretized via the control volume technique. For this end, the second-order upwind and second-order central difference method are used respectively to discretize the convection and diffusion terms of governing equations. Also, the Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure Linked Equation (SIMPLE) is considered for coupling of the pressure and velocity. It is noteworthy that the convergence criterion to stop the numerical iterations for the velocity and continuity is set on 10−5, and for the energy on 10−6.

To find the best mesh structure with the highest accuracy besides the lowest computational cost, the change of the pressure drop and average outlet temperature of both the fluids are examined with the changes of element number. According to the results, which are depicted in Table 4 , it is clear that with increasing the element number further than 6744414, the pressure drop and temperature do not change considerably. Hence, the mentioned element number is chosen for the numerical investigation of this microchannel heat exchanger. Fig. 2 depicts the mesh for the geometry. The structured mesh method is utilized for the microchannels, while the initial and final areas are based on the unstructured tetrahedron method. Also, for the more accurate result, the number of cells at the input and output areas of the microchannels is small enough due to intense gradients.

To trust the authenticity of the utilized numerical method, the pressure drop at different Reynolds numbers as well as the hot fluid temperature profile along the middle channel at Re = 250 for a microchannel heat exchanger working with water, as the hot fluid and cold fluid, are compared with those reported by Zhou et al. [7]. The microchannels' cross-section is 0.4 × 2 mm, and the gap between two successive microchannels is 1 mm. Also, the inlet temperatures for the warm and cool liquids are 353 K and 295 K, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 3 , the numerical results are perfectly fitted to the results of Zhou et al. [7], which thoroughly validates the used numerical method.

This research evaluates the hydrothermal features of a counter-flow microchannel heat exchanger working with the water–GQDs nanofluid as the warm fluid and water as the cold fluid. The nanofluid concentrations utilized are 0, 0.1%, 0.3%, and 0.5%, and the inlet temperatures of the warm and cold fluids are 313 K and 283 K, respectively. This study is performed at Reynolds numbers of 50, 100, 150, and 200 for the warm fluid, and 100 for the cool fluid. It is worth mentioning that since the Re and type of the cold fluid are invariant, the following reports about the Re and concentration only refer to the hot fluid.

To understand the flow pattern, Fig. 4 shows the streamlines and vectors as well as the velocity contours at Re = 200 and φ = 0.5%. The colors of the vectors and streamlines are based on the temperature. As the fluid passes through the microchannels, heat is transferred from the warm fluid to the cold liquid, and finally, passing through the end area, it exits the two outputs embedded at the end of the heat exchanger (also see Fig. 1). According to the vectors’ colors, it is observed that the nanofluid is at high temperature at the inlet of the microchannels, while its temperature is much lower when leaving the microchannels. Also, based on the velocity contour, it is observed that the regions with the lowest velocity relate to the two corners of the initial area as well as between the two successive channels at the outlet of the microchannels. Moreover, according to the magnified part, the maximum velocity is related to the center of each microchannel.

Due to the temperature dependence of the thermal conductivity and viscosity, the amount of these properties at a specific concentration is not uniform and varies with temperature. Therefore, Fig. 5 is depicted to present the distribution of the thermal conductivity and viscosity at Re = 200 and φ = 0.5%. By passing the hot fluid through the microchannels and reducing its temperature, the thermal conductivity decreases while the viscosity increases. The dependence of the thermal conductivity on temperature leads to a more convective heat transfer coefficient in those regions with higher temperatures because of the higher thermal conductivity. Also, due to the inverse relationship of temperature and viscosity, in the areas with higher temperatures, the fluid’s viscosity is lower, leading to a decrease in the hydrodynamic resistance of fluid movement.

In Fig. 6 , the heat transfer rate from the hot fluid to the cold fluid for different Re values and concentrations in two cases of variable and constant thermophysical properties (c.t.p.) has been plotted. It should be noted that in the case of constant properties, the thermophysical properties are considered at the mean temperature of the inlet and outlet. It is perceived that as the concentration elevates, the heat transfer rate enhances. Two reasons can explain this phenomenon. Firstly, by increasing the concentration, the fluid's thermal conductivity improves, resulting in a more convective heat transfer coefficient. Secondly, since adding the nanoparticles to the base fluid increases the kinematic viscosity, the hot fluid's velocity must be increased to keep the Re constant, which leads to an enhancement in the heat transfer rate. Another noteworthy point is that adding the nanoparticles to the host liquid has a more significant impact on the heat transfer amount at lower Re values. So that at Re = 50, adding the nanoparticles with the concentration of 0.5% to the base fluid causes a 13% rise in the heat transfer rate, while this value reduces to 8% at Re = 200. This is because at higher Re values, the heat transfer rate is high due to the great mass flow rate, so that suspending the nanoparticles in the host liquid leads to less improvement in the amount of heat transfer rate. However, as the flow rate is fewer at lower Re values, the fluid inertia is minor, and hence, the augmentation in the penetration of the heat caused by adding the nanoparticles is more noticeable, resulting in further enhancement in the heat transfer rate. Moreover, it is seen that the heat transfer rate improves by rising the Re, which is owing to the increased fluid displacement and reduced boundary layer thickness. In addition to the mentioned points, comparing the heat transfer rates in the cases of variable and constant thermophysical properties shows a significant difference between the results, in a way that at φ = 0.5% and Re = 200, there is a 12% difference between the heat transfer values. This difference indicates the importance of considering variable thermophysical properties in this simulation. It should be mentioned that all the following results in this paper are based on the variable thermophysical properties.

In Fig. 6, the change of the nanofluid velocity with the concentration variation was mentioned. To precisely investigate the effect of the concentration on the velocity distribution, Fig. 7 is presented, demonstrating the velocity profile for the warm and cold fluids along the centerline of the middle microchannel at Re = 200 and different concentrations. According to the profiles, in the first quarter of the microchannel length, the velocity magnitude changes significantly owing to the growth of the boundary layer, while apart from this region, the value of velocity is approximately constant, which indicates the development of the hydrodynamic boundary layer. It is also observed that by increasing the nanofluid concentration, the velocity of the hot fluid rises, while the cold fluid velocity decreases. The reason is that by augmenting the concentration, the amount of intensification in the viscosity is more intense than the amount of intensification in the density so that to keep the Re constant, the value of the velocity must be increased. On the other hand, according to Fig. 6, the heat transfer rate from the warm fluid to the cold liquid promotes by increasing the concentration. As the heat transfer rises, the temperature of the cold fluid elevates more during the microchannel, which causes a decline in the viscosity of the cold liquid, resulting in a decrement in the amount of cold liquid velocity required to keep the Re constant. It is worth mentioning that the changes in the hot liquid’s velocity with the nanofluid concentration are more significant than those in the cold fluid’s velocity.

Fig. 8 shows the nanofluid and cold fluid velocities in different microchannels at Re = 200 and φ = 0.5%. It should be noted that in this figure, numbering the microchannels is done from the middle channel, so that the microchannel No. 1 is related to the middle channel and the microchannel No. 15 is related to the side channel. Due to the symmetry of the geometry, the velocity distribution in only half of the microchannels is shown. For both of the fluids, the mean velocity in the side microchannels is more than that in the middle microchannels, which causes more contribution of the side channels in the heat transfer rate. Indeed, this is owing to the location of the outlets that have been placed on the sides of the heat exchanger. This means that the stream passes the side channels simply, while there is further resistance at the end of the middle channels. Another noticeable point in this figure is that the velocity distribution for the cold fluid is more uniform than that for the hot fluid so that for the hot fluid, the velocity of the side microchannel is 4.8% higher than that of the middle microchannel, while this difference for the cold fluid is 2.9%. This is because due to the higher Re of the hot fluid, in this case, the velocity and momentum of the hot fluid are higher, resulting in a more uneven distribution of the velocity.

In Fig. 9 , the hot fluid streamlines at varied Reynolds numbers for φ = 0.5% have been drawn. As can be seen, at Re = 50 and Re = 100, except at the microchannels’ outlet, no vortex is formed in other areas. At the outlet of the microchannels, since the flow passes from the small cross-sectional area to the large final area, eddy current forms. These eddies are greatened with the Re increment because, at higher Reynolds numbers, the stream enters the final area faster, creating dead zones between two consecutive microchannels wider, and leading to the formation of larger vortices. At Re = 150 and 200, besides the outlet regions of the microchannels, there are also vortices at the corners of the initial area due to the separation of the flow, a phenomenon that does not happen at lower Re values.

Fig. 10 presents the temperature profile along the middle microchannel for the warm and cold liquids at Re = 200 and different concentrations. As each profile shows, at the thermal developing regions, the temperature varies with a non-uniform slope up to the fully-developed area. After that, it changes at a constant slope. It is also seen that as the concentration rises, the difference between the inlet and outlet temperatures of the hot fluid decreases slightly. The reason is that according to Fig. 7, the flow rate of the warm fluid augments by increasing the concentration so that the residence time of the hot fluid diminishes, resulting in a lower temperature change. On the other hand, it is found that as the concentration increases, the temperature change in the cold fluid increases. This is because as the concentration rises, the heat transfer rate promotes, and the cold fluid flow rate decreases, leading to an increase in residence time, which results in more temperature changes of the cold fluid.

Fig. 11 reveals the contour of the temperature at the vertical cross-section in the center part of the middle microchannel for Re = 200 and different concentrations. It is found that by rising the concentration, the uniformity of the temperature uplifts, so that the difference between the highest and lowest temperatures is the lowest in the case of employing the 0.5% nanofluid. The reason for this observation is that firstly in the case of using the more concentrated nanofluid, the thermal conductivity promotes, leading to more heat penetration between fluid layers, and secondly, by utilizing the nanofluid at higher concentrations, the fluid velocity and consequently the heat transfer rate augments, resulting in the more temperature uniformity.

In Fig. 12 , the iso-temperature surfaces related to 305 K for the warm liquid and 290 K for the cold liquid are presented at φ = 0.5% and different Reynolds numbers. These surfaces divide the hot part of the heat exchanger into two zones with the temperature above 305 K and below 305 K, and the cold part into two zones with the temperature above 290 K and below 290 K. It is observed that at Re = 50, a large area of the hot fluid is below 305 K. This region is shortened with the Reynolds number increment so that at Re = 200, more than half of the hot fluid is at or above 305 K. To justify this phenomenon, it is noteworthy that at low Reynolds numbers, the hot fluid flow rate is low so that it has sufficient opportunity to exchange heat with the cold fluid, and thus, its temperature decreases rapidly. However, at high Reynolds numbers, the hot fluid flow rate is higher, and therefore, the residence time of the hot fluid is low that leads to the decrease in the temperature change along with the heat exchanger, and consequently, most of the heat exchanger is above 305 K. Also, it can be observed from the cold fluid iso-surface that for Re = 200 (i.e., hot fluid Reynolds number), a large area of the cold fluid is above 290 K, however by decreasing the Reynolds number, this area shrinks so that at Re = 50, only the end parts of the heat exchanger are at or above 290 K. This is because as the Reynolds number augments, the heat transfer rate enhances due to more flow rate of the hot fluid, and therefore, more heat is transferred to the cold fluid. Also, as mentioned before, with the hot fluid Re increment, the cold fluid velocity decreases that causes more residence time of the cold fluid. The more residence time of the cold fluid, as well as the more heat transfer to the cold fluid, leads to the increase in the variation of the cold fluid temperature so that its temperature changes from 283 K to 290 K in the initial areas of the heat exchanger. On the contrary, at lower Re values, since the heat transfer is fewer, the change of the cold fluid temperature is minor, and the fluid reaches 290 K in the final zones of the heat exchanger.

The iso-temperature surfaces at 305 K and 290 K, respectively for the warm liquid and cold liquid, are demonstrated in Fig. 13 at Re = 200 and various concentrations. Evidently, with suspending the nanoparticles in the host liquid, due to the augmentation of the flow rate and the reduction of the fluid residence time, the hot fluid iso-temperature surface moves towards the downstream. Moreover, in the case of utilizing the nanofluid as the hot fluid, the cold fluid iso-temperature surface is moved towards the upstream, which is due to the increment of the heat transfer from the warm liquid to the cold liquid as well as the more residence time of the cold liquid.

Fig. 14 is illustrated here to appraise the temperature contours in the hot and cold cupric solid parts of the heat exchanger at different Reynolds numbers and concentrations. As is seen, by elevating either the Re or φ, the temperature of both the hot and cold solid parts intensifies. This phenomenon is because of the enhancement in the energy transfer from the warm fluid to the cold liquid by increasing the Re or φ (see Fig. 6). Indeed, by increasing either the Re or φ, the heat transfer rate enhances, and the solid parts convey more heat, resulting in higher temperatures of the solid parts. One crucial point is that the temperature rise is more with the Re increment compared to the concentration enhancement, which is related to the more significant impact of the Re increment on the heat transfer enhancement.

To assess the variation of the overall heat transfer coefficient versus the concentration and Re, Fig. 15 is presented. It is seen that by rising the Re, the overall heat transfer coefficient improves. Indeed, by the Re increment, the convection of the fluid intensifies, and consequently, the convective heat transfer coefficient and the overall heat transfer coefficient improve. In addition, it is observed that utilizing the nanofluid at greater concentrations promotes the overall heat transfer coefficient for all the Reynolds numbers. This is because suspending the nanoscale particles in the host liquid not only enhances the heat penetration but also augments the hot fluid velocity. Both of these phenomena improve the convective heat transfer coefficient, and hence, the overall heat transfer coefficient increases.

In Fig. 16 , the heat exchanger’s effectiveness has been illustrated at various Re values and concentrations. It can be seen that for Re = 50 and Re = 100, via elevating the concentration, the effectiveness declines. In fact, in these Re values, the minimum heat capacity rate is related to the warm fluid, and thus, the effectiveness is evaluated by Eq. (18). According to this equation, the value of the effectiveness is proportional to the warm fluid’s temperature change. Based on Table 5 , which is related to the temperature change of the warm and cold fluids at different Re values and concentrations, the temperature change of the warm fluid decreases by increasing the concentration, and consequently, the value of effectiveness declines. On the other hand, it is observed that for Re = 150 and Re = 200, the effectiveness has an increasing trend versus the concentration. Indeed, since the minimum heat capacity rate in the mentioned Reynolds numbers is related to the cold fluid, Eq. (19) describes the value of effectiveness that is proportional to the temperature change of the cold fluid. According to Table 5, the amount of cold fluid’s temperature change increases by augmenting the concentration, leading to an improved effectiveness. Additionally, it is noticeable that at all the concentrations, by increasing the Re from 50 to 150, the effectiveness decreases, and then by rising the Re to 200, it rises. The reason for this can be explained by the changes in the hot and cold fluid temperatures. As the Re grows from 50 to 100, the temperature difference of the hot fluid decreases at all the concentrations. Since in these Re values, the effectiveness is calculated by Eq. (18), the effectiveness reduces. On the other hand, at Re = 150, the effectiveness is a function of the cold fluid temperature. Based on Table 5, at all the concentrations, the magnitude of the cold fluid temperature change at Re = 150 is less than the hot fluid temperature change at Re = 100. Thus, the effectiveness decreases as the Re increases to 150. Eventually, at Re = 200, the effectiveness is dependent on the amount of cold fluid’s temperature change, and since the value of the cold fluid’s temperature change at Re = 200 is more than that at Re = 150, the effectiveness improves as the Re elevates from 150 to 200. It is noteworthy that despite the effectiveness decreases by suspending the nanoscale particles in the host fluid at Re = 50 and Re = 100, its value at all the concentrations is higher than that at Re = 150 and Re = 200.

The values of the NTU parameter are illustrated in Table 6 at various concentrations and Re values. It is perceived that for the base fluid and nanofluid at all the concentrations, with the increment in the Re from 50 to 150, the NTU decreases, and then by increasing more to 200, its value increases. This observation can be justified via the changes in the overall heat transfer coefficient and minimum heat capacity rate. The amount of the overall heat transfer coefficient at all the concentrations enhances with the Re increment. However, by augmenting the Re to 150, the minimum heat capacity rate increases, and after that, it decreases. In fact, since the U and Cmin are respectively in the numerator and denominator of the Eq. (20), the NTU decreases as the Re goes up from 50 to 150 and then rises as the Re reaches 200. Also, it should be noted that at Re = 150 and Re = 200, increasing the concentration leads to the higher NTU, while it does not have a specified trend at Re = 50 and Re = 100. This is because, at Re = 150 and Re = 200, by the concentration increment, the value of U enhances while Cmin decreases, which both of these changes are in accordance with the NTU enhancement. However, at Re = 50 and Re = 100, with the concentration increment, both the U and Cmin increase, and since the increment of U is in accordance with the NTU enhancement, and the increase in the Cmin is against the NTU enhancement, so the NTU fluctuates with the concentration rise at the mentioned Re values. Eventually, the NTU values demonstrate that for all the concentrations, the values of NTU at Re = 50 and Re = 100 are higher than those at Re = 150 and Re = 200.

Adding the nanoparticles to the host liquid, along with uplifting the thermal performance, can lead to adverse effects such as increased pressure drop. As the pressure drop increases, the power required to pump the fluid augments, leading to an increase in the energy consumed by the heat exchanger. Given this, the evaluation of hydrodynamic parameters, along with thermal studies, is of great importance and needs to be analyzed. In this regard, Fig. 17 shows the pressure contours for the nanofluid stream (hot fluid) at various particle concentrations and Re values. It is observed that with increasing the Re, the pressure drop increases because of the velocity gradient intensification on the walls. Moreover, it is perceived that the pressure loss intensifies by the increment of the nanoparticle concentration. Two factors can explain this phenomenon; firstly, as the concentration increases, the viscosity of the fluid augments, which increases the friction, and as a result, the pressure drop grows, and secondly, as mentioned before, by adding the nanoparticles at a constant Re, the velocity increases, which heightens the velocity gradients, leading to the augmentation of the pressure drop.

Fig. 18 exhibits the cold fluid pressure drop versus the hot fluid Re at various concentrations. The cold fluid pressure drop decreases by increasing the Re and concentration. This observation can be explained by the change in the velocity and viscosity of the cold fluid due to the change at the hot fluid Re and concentration. As Fig. 6 shows, by increasing the Re and concentration, the heat transfer rate increases, which causes the decrement in the viscosity and velocity of the cold fluid. This reduction in the velocity and viscosity causes fewer friction and velocity gradients in the cold fluid, results in a lower cold fluid pressure loss. Another noticeable point is that the cold fluid pressure drop decreases more with the Re increment than the concentration elevation, so that by adding 0.5% nanoparticles to the host fluid at Re = 200, the pressure drop decreases 5.7%, while increasing the Re from 50 to 200 at φ = 0.5%, reduces the pressure drop 24%. The reason is that the velocity and viscosity of the cold fluid change more with the Re compared to the concentration.

Since the pressure drop of the warm fluid and cool fluid increases and decreases respectively with the Re and concentration increment, to assess the whole pressure drop, the summation of the warm and cool fluids’ pressure drops has been presented in Table 7 . It is evident that the whole pressure loss intensifies by the Re and concentration increment, which indicates that the increase in the hot fluid pressure drop is more significant than the decrease in the cold fluid pressure drop. In fact, by increasing either the Re or concentration, the amount of increase in viscosity and velocity of the hot fluid is greater than the amount of decrease in velocity and viscosity of the cold fluid, which leads to an increase in the whole pressure loss.

So far, the thermal and hydraulic attributes of the microchannel heat exchanger have been investigated. The obtained outcomes unveiled that in the case of using the nanofluid, the heat transfer rate enhances with the penalty of further pressure drop. Increasing the heat transfer rate is a desirable result, while augmenting the pressure drop is an adverse effect. In this regard, to evaluate the merit of utilizing the nanofluid in this microchannel heat exchanger, the performance index is utilized (Eq. (21)), which indicates the ratio of increased heat transfer rate to the pressure drop augmentation. Fig. 19 exhibits the performance index at varied Re values and concentrations. Obviously, at Re = 50 and Re = 100, the performance index for all the concentrations is upper than unity, while for Re = 150 and Re = 200, it is below one. This reveals that by adding the particles to the host liquid at Re = 50 and Re = 100, the heat transfer rate increases more than the pressure loss, while at Re = 150 Re = 200, the increase in the pressure drop is greater than that in the heat transfer rate. Moreover, the highest amount of the performance index is related to Re = 50 and φ = 0.5%. According to the results, utilizing the nanofluids at Re = 50 and Re = 100 is justified and recommended. In contrast, at Re = 150 and Re = 200, the use of the nanofluids is not suitable due to the high pressure drop penalty.

In this paper, for the first time, the hydrothermal characteristics of a microchannel heat exchanger operating with the novel graphene quantum dots nanofluid as the warm fluid and water as the cold fluid were assessed. The results unveiled that the use of nanofluids improves the heat transfer rate by a maximum of 13% for only an 8% pressure drop increment. Moreover, it was found that in the case of using water or nanofluid, the effectiveness and the number of transfer units at Reynolds numbers of 50 and 100 are higher than those at Reynolds numbers of 150 and 200. The evaluation of the performance index demonstrated that at Reynolds numbers of 50 and 100, contrary to Reynolds numbers of 150 and 200, adding the nanoscale particles to the host liquid increases the heat transfer rate more than the pressure loss. Also, the highest performance index was related to the 0.5% nanofluid at Reynolds number of 50. Based on the evaluated results, it is concluded that employing the nanofluid at Reynolds numbers of 50 and 100 leads to a much better heat exchanger performance, while at Reynolds numbers 150 and 200, utilizing the nanofluid is not recommended due to the high pressure loss penalty, low effectiveness, and low number of transfer unit.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
